It relies on the Gri"on JScript backdoor Attackers use these interpreters to execute malicious commands on compromised systems.

Cobalt/Meterpreter This technique is used in conjunction with Cobalt Strike (Cobalt/Meterpreter) to exploit vulnerabilities and execute payloads on targeted systems, especially in post-exploitation scenarios.

PowerShell Empire Attackers use these interpreters to execute malicious commands on compromised systems.

spear-phishing campaigns These groups use effective spear-phishing campaigns with malicious Microsoft Office documents to deliver their payloads.

well-known Microsoft O"ice exploits generated by their exploitation framework The use of well-known Microsoft Office exploits generated by exploitation frameworks is a common tactic. These exploits do not require zero-day vulnerabilities but instead rely on the fact that many systems remain unpatched against older, well-documented flaws.

they use documents with exploits Documents with exploits are used to execute malicious code on the victim's system. These documents typically exploit vulnerabilities in software like Microsoft Office to gain initial access.

that !rst load a small downloader and then a Cobalt beacon. The initial exploit is designed to load a small downloader, which then downloads additional malicious components (such as the Cobalt beacon) from an external server.

JS sni"ing (MageCarting) in order to obtain data about payment cards directly from websites JS snifing (MageCarting) involves injecting malicious JavaScript into a website, often through compromised third-party services or direct website exploits. This allows attackers to intercept and steal payment card data directly from users interacting with the website.
